key_insight: The paper shows that two-dimensional polycrystalline disk packings can be made both stealthy and hyperuniform by design, unlike ordinary polycrystals, because long-range grain correlations suppress large-scale density fluctuations.
engineering_relevance: These structures could enable metamaterials with unusually transparent optical response and improved diffusion transport, useful for nanolithography- or 3D-printing-based fabrication.
method: Generated 2D identical-disk packings using collective-coordinate optimization to enforce stealthy hyperuniformity (SHU).
Compared SHU packings against modified Lubachevsky–Stillinger rapid-compression polycrystals (LS) as a nonhyperuniform reference.
Evaluated two-point statistics, grain size, specific surface area, diffusion spreadability, and effective dielectric response.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an extensive comparison showing SHU packings have distinct grain-size distributions and, in strong-contrast expansion, are perfectly transparent to small-wave-vector electromagnetic waves, while LS packings are not.
watch_item: The SHU microstructures are not expected to form spontaneously, so practical use depends on precise controlled fabrication rather than self-assembly.
paper_title: Two-dimensional stealthy hyperuniform polycrystalline disk packings
